one_line_takeaway: A design paper that repurposes attack-side LLM profiling (PVP/E-PhishGEN) into a defensive PVP→risk-scenario→PDP→online-agent pipeline for protecting university faculty from Whaling, validated only by a qualitative two-person demonstration with no quantitative benchmarking.
key_finding: The paper reports only qualitative, anecdotal outcomes from a two-participant demonstration: the online agent flagged synthetic emails impersonating research-funding procedures or internal IT as suspicious/highly suspicious and recommended out-of-band verification, while classifying a benign op-ed introduction email to participant B as low-to-moderate risk with appropriate precautions. The authors conclude the PDP-plus-online-agent combination could produce risk judgments and explanations "consistent with the work context" of faculty targets, even without disclosing detailed PVPs. No accuracy, detection-rate, false-positive, or comparative numbers are given for the proposed system. The striking figures appearing in the text (e.g., 101 participants and 12/54/54/56% click-through rates, 88% profiles rated useful; 240M emails with >80% targeting Japanese speakers) are all cited results from Heiding et al. [17] and Proofpoint [16] respectively, NOT results produced by this paper.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a defensive framework against Whaling (a form of spear phishing targeting high-authority individuals) tailored to university faculty members at Japanese universities. Its own novelty is inverting two cited attack-oriented frameworks — Heiding et al.'s Personalized Vulnerability Profiles (PVPs) [17] and Pajola et al.'s E-PhishGEN scenario generation [5] — into a defensive pipeline of four LLM-agent stages: (i) a PVP-generation agent that builds a vulnerability profile from OSINT, (ii) a risk-scenario generation agent, (iii) a PDP-generation agent that produces a Personalized Defense Profile combining public info with internal institutional attributes (roles, approval chains, access rights), and (iv) an online analysis agent that uses the PDP as its system prompt to risk-assess incoming email. Additional contributions are university-faculty-specific PVP/PDP item designs (positions/authority, research funding, human networks, work cycles) and concrete JSON schemas (given in full in the appendix) plus the four agents' system prompts. NOTE: the PVP concept and E-PhishGEN belong to cited prior work; the inversion into defense and the university adaptation are this paper's own.
threat_model: Whaling — spear phishing aimed at high-value, high-authority individuals (here, Japanese university faculty who combine research leadership with institutional decision authority over budgets, approvals, and system administration). The adversary leverages the unusually large public information exposure of academics (faculty pages, DBLP/Google Scholar/researchmap, public funding award databases, social media) together with generative AI to build high-precision target profiles and craft fluent, personalized fraudulent emails (impersonating IT/CSIRT, research-support offices, funding administrators, collaborators) to induce fraudulent wire transfers, credential/sensitive-data disclosure, or improper approvals. The paper notes such tailored, low-volume emails evade traditional pattern/ML detectors. A secondary threat the paper acknowledges but does not evaluate is prompt-injection against the defense agent, since it processes untrusted email content.
limitations: Self-stated: validation limited to a preliminary study of only two participants (both from the authors' group), so broader coverage across faculty roles/domains is needed; internal attributes (positions, approval authority, workflows) were captured via ad hoc interviews and lack standardized representations for scale; the online phase was tested on only a small set of sample emails with no systematic benchmarking on real mail traffic or diverse simulated Whaling; no field experiment with real phishing delivery was done for ethical reasons; and practical CSIRT/mail-infrastructure deployment raises unaddressed issues (workflow integration, decision-log governance, privacy/data management). Reviewer-added: no quantitative metrics, no baseline comparison, and prompt-injection robustness is asserted as important but untested.

datasets_used: No standard/benchmark dataset. OSINT inputs for participant A only: university faculty profile page, prior-affiliation researcher pages, lab member lists, DBLP, Google Scholar, researchmap, a funding-agency award database, public social media accounts
Hand-constructed sample emails: benign business-like messages plus synthetic emails impersonating research-funding administrators / internal IT (not a released corpus)
quantitative_results: No quantitative metrics reported for the proposed system; evaluation is a preliminary qualitative study with n=2 faculty participants (A and B) using categorical risk labels (safe/caution/dangerous) only
No accuracy, detection rate, false-positive/false-negative, or comparative numbers are given

(Context, cited work only — NOT this paper's results: Heiding et al. [17] 101 participants, click-through 12%/54%/54%/56% across four email types, 88% profiles useful, 4% inaccurate; Proofpoint [16] ~240M fraudulent emails in May 2025, >80% targeting Japanese speakers)
baselines_compared: None — the paper performs no baseline or comparative evaluation against existing phishing/Whaling detectors
